content.text: Why Flat Chunking Breaks on Long Documents
Flat chunking splits a document into fixed-size windows and embeds each independently. This works well when information is local. But documents with global structure expose a fundamental weakness.

Consider a 50-page earnings report that produces 300 chunks. When a user asks what drove revenue growth this quarter, the relevant signal is scattered across the revenue section, CEO commentary, and forward guidance. No single chunk is complete. The retriever picks whichever chunk has the highest cosine similarity to the query, which may be a narrow data point rather than the synthesised picture.
Asking for the main themes of a document requires synthesising dozens of chunks, but only 1-5 are retrieved at query time.
Connecting a cause in Section 2 with an effect in Section 8 is impossible if neither chunk mentions the other.
The retriever treats a title, a paragraph, and an appendix row as equally atomic units.

RAPTOR (Recursive Abstractive Processing for Tree-Organised Retrieval) solves this by building a multi-level tree of summaries over the original chunks. Queries can match at any level of the tree -- from a root-level overview down to a specific leaf chunk -- letting a single retriever handle both broad and precise queries.

attrs.alt: RAPTOR tree structure: raw chunks at Level 0, cluster summaries at Level 1, root summary at Level 2. Build process: cluster with UMAP+GMM, summarise with LLM, recurse until one node remains. Query-time: collapsed tree search returns relevant nodes from all levels simultaneously.

content.text: The build process is a bottom-up recursion. Each pass reduces the number of nodes by clustering them and replacing each cluster with an LLM-written summary. The recursion terminates when a single node (the root) remains.

Start with N text chunks. Embed each chunk to get a high-dimensional vector (e.g. 1536 dimensions with text-embedding-3-small). High-dimensional cosine distances are noisy -- clusters are not well-separated. RAPTOR uses UMAP to project down to 2 dimensions before clustering.

import numpy as np
from umap import UMAP

def reduce_embeddings(embeddings: list[list[float]], n_components: int = 2) -> np.ndarray:
    arr = np.array(embeddings)
    # n_neighbors controls local vs global structure trade-off.
    # min_dist=0 allows tighter clusters.
    reducer = UMAP(n_components=n_components, n_neighbors=10, min_dist=0.0, random_state=42)
    return reducer.fit_transform(arr)
Step 2 - Soft Cluster with a Gaussian Mixture Model

A hard clustering algorithm like k-means assigns each point to exactly one cluster. RAPTOR uses a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) instead, which produces a probability distribution over clusters for each point. A chunk about quarterly revenue might belong to both the financial performance cluster and the CEO commentary cluster with non-zero probability.
This soft assignment means a chunk can be included in the summary of more than one cluster, which prevents information loss at cluster boundaries.

from sklearn.mixture import GaussianMixture

def cluster_embeddings(
    reduced: np.ndarray,
    max_clusters: int = 50,
    threshold: float = 0.5,
) -> list[list[int]]:
    """Return clusters as lists of chunk indices (soft-assigned)."""
    n = len(reduced)
    # BIC selects the number of components automatically.
    best_bic, best_n = float("inf"), 1
    for k in range(1, min(max_clusters, n) + 1):
        gm = GaussianMixture(n_components=k, random_state=42)
        gm.fit(reduced)
        bic = gm.bic(reduced)
        if bic < best_bic:
            best_bic, best_n = bic, k

    gm = GaussianMixture(n_components=best_n, random_state=42).fit(reduced)
    probs = gm.predict_proba(reduced)  # shape (n_chunks, n_clusters)

    # Each cluster gets all chunks whose probability exceeds the threshold.
    clusters = []
    for k in range(best_n):
        members = [i for i in range(n) if probs[i, k] >= threshold]
        if members:
            clusters.append(members)
    return clusters
Step 3 - Summarise Each Cluster with an LLM

Concatenate the texts in each cluster and ask an LLM to write a 2-4 sentence summary. The summary becomes a new node at the next level of the tree. Its embedding is computed so it can be clustered again in the next recursion.

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

_SUMMARY_PROMPT = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system",
     "You are a concise technical summariser. Synthesise the key points"
     " from the passages below into 2-4 sentences."
     " Do not add information not present in the passages."),
    ("human", "{passages}"),
])
_llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)

async def summarise_cluster(texts: list[str]) -> str:
    combined = "\n\n---\n\n".join(texts)
    msg = await (_SUMMARY_PROMPT | _llm).ainvoke({"passages": combined})
    return msg.content.strip()
Step 4 - Recurse Until One Node Remains

After each pass you have a set of summary nodes. Embed them and repeat the cluster-and-summarise loop. The recursion terminates when clustering returns a single cluster (all remaining nodes are similar enough to collapse into one root summary).

import asyncio
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

@dataclass
class RaptorNode:
    text: str
    level: int
    embedding: list[float] = field(default_factory=list)
    children: list["RaptorNode"] = field(default_factory=list)

async def build_raptor_tree(
    chunks: list[str],
    embedder: OpenAIEmbeddings,
    max_levels: int = 5,
) -> tuple[list[RaptorNode], RaptorNode]:
    """Return (all_nodes_across_levels, root_node)."""
    all_nodes: list[RaptorNode] = []

    # Level 0: raw chunks
    embeddings = await embedder.aembed_documents(chunks)
    current_nodes = [
        RaptorNode(text=txt, level=0, embedding=emb)
        for txt, emb in zip(chunks, embeddings)
    ]
    all_nodes.extend(current_nodes)

    level = 1
    while len(current_nodes) > 1 and level <= max_levels:
        reduced = reduce_embeddings([n.embedding for n in current_nodes])
        clusters = cluster_embeddings(reduced)

        if len(clusters) == 1:
            # All nodes collapsed -- we are at the root.
            break

        # Summarise each cluster in parallel.
        summaries = await asyncio.gather(*[
            summarise_cluster([current_nodes[i].text for i in cluster])
            for cluster in clusters
        ])
        summary_embeddings = await embedder.aembed_documents(list(summaries))

        next_nodes = []
        for summary_text, emb, cluster in zip(summaries, summary_embeddings, clusters):
            node = RaptorNode(
                text=summary_text,
                level=level,
                embedding=emb,
                children=[current_nodes[i] for i in cluster],
            )
            next_nodes.append(node)

        all_nodes.extend(next_nodes)
        current_nodes = next_nodes
        level += 1

    # Final single-node root summary.
    if len(current_nodes) > 1:
        root_text = await summarise_cluster([n.text for n in current_nodes])
        root_emb = await embedder.aembed_documents([root_text])
        root = RaptorNode(text=root_text, level=level, embedding=root_emb[0],
                          children=current_nodes)
    else:
        root = current_nodes[0]

    all_nodes.append(root)
    return all_nodes, root
The tree structure enables two complementary retrieval strategies. Both work with the same index -- all nodes across all levels are stored in one vector store.

Index every node (leaf and summary) in a flat vector store. At query time, embed the query and return the top-k nodes across all levels by cosine similarity, regardless of which level they sit at. This is the default and works well for most queries.
Questions about overall themes or summaries tend to match high-level summary nodes whose language is abstract and synthesised.
Questions asking for a specific metric or fact tend to match Level 0 leaf chunks that contain the exact number.
Returning a mix of levels is often better than forcing all results to the same level.

from langchain_core.vectorstores import VectorStore

def build_collapsed_index(
    all_nodes: list[RaptorNode],
    vector_store: VectorStore,
) -> VectorStore:
    """Add every node from all levels to a single flat index."""
    texts = [n.text for n in all_nodes]
    embeddings = [n.embedding for n in all_nodes]
    metadatas = [{"level": n.level} for n in all_nodes]
    vector_store.add_embeddings(list(zip(texts, embeddings)), metadatas=metadatas)
    return vector_store

def collapsed_tree_search(
    query_embedding: list[float],
    vector_store: VectorStore,
    k: int = 5,
) -> list[str]:
    docs = vector_store.similarity_search_by_vector(query_embedding, k=k)
    return [d.page_content for d in docs]

An alternative strategy traverses the tree top-down. Start at the root, score all children of the current node, keep the top-d by cosine similarity, then recurse into those children. This is useful when you want explicitly hierarchical retrieval -- for example, when you know the query targets a specific sub-section of a long document.

import numpy as np

def cosine_similarity(a: list[float], b: list[float]) -> float:
    a, b = np.array(a), np.array(b)
    return float(np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b) + 1e-9))

def tree_traversal_search(
    query_embedding: list[float],
    root: RaptorNode,
    beam_width: int = 2,
    max_depth: int = 3,
) -> list[RaptorNode]:
    """Greedy top-down traversal; returns nodes at the deepest reached level."""
    current_level = [root]
    for _ in range(max_depth):
        if not any(n.children for n in current_level):
            break  # Reached leaves.
        # Expand and score all children.
        candidates = [child for node in current_level for child in node.children]
        scored = sorted(
            candidates,
            key=lambda n: cosine_similarity(query_embedding, n.embedding),
            reverse=True,
        )
        current_level = scored[:beam_width]
    return current_level

Wrapping RAPTOR as a LangChain BaseRetriever makes it composable with any LCEL chain. The retriever stores the pre-built all_nodes list and query embedder, then dispatches to collapsed tree search on each call.

from langchain_core.retrievers import BaseRetriever
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from langchain_core.callbacks import CallbackManagerForRetrieverRun
from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

class RaptorRetriever(BaseRetriever):
    vector_store: FAISS
    embedder: OpenAIEmbeddings
    k: int = 5

    @classmethod
    async def from_texts(
        cls,
        chunks: list[str],
        embedder: OpenAIEmbeddings,
        k: int = 5,
    ) -> "RaptorRetriever":
        all_nodes, _ = await build_raptor_tree(chunks, embedder)
        texts = [n.text for n in all_nodes]
        embeddings = [n.embedding for n in all_nodes]
        metadatas = [{"level": n.level} for n in all_nodes]
        vs = await FAISS.afrom_embeddings(
            list(zip(texts, embeddings)),
            embedding=embedder,
            metadatas=metadatas,
        )
        return cls(vector_store=vs, embedder=embedder, k=k)

    def _get_relevant_documents(
        self,
        query: str,
        *,
        run_manager: CallbackManagerForRetrieverRun,
    ) -> list[Document]:
        return self.vector_store.similarity_search(query, k=self.k)

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

async def build_raptor_rag(chunks: list[str]):
    embedder = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
    retriever = await RaptorRetriever.from_texts(chunks, embedder, k=5)

    prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
        ("system",
         "Answer the question using only the context below.\n\nContext:\n{context}"),
        ("human", "{question}"),
    ])
    llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)

    def format_docs(docs):
        return "\n\n".join(d.page_content for d in docs)

    return (
        {"context": retriever | format_docs, "question": RunnablePassthrough()}
        | prompt
        | llm
        | StrOutputParser()
    )

# Usage:
# import asyncio
# chain = asyncio.run(build_raptor_rag(document_chunks))
# answer = chain.invoke("What drove revenue growth this quarter?")
The primary cost of RAPTOR is at index-build time: every cluster at every level requires one LLM summarisation call.
is the number of clusters at level

. In the best case, clusters shrink geometrically (each cluster merges 3-5 nodes), so total calls are roughly O(N / fan-in) -- about 20-30% of N for a fan-in of 4-5. In the worst case, each level reduces by only one node (O(N) calls).
RAPTOR adds real value in specific scenarios. Deploying it everywhere is wasteful -- it trades index-build LLM calls and engineering complexity for better broad-query recall.
Documents are long (10+ pages), contain multiple independently-addressable sections, and users ask both high-level and detail-level questions.
Documents are short, queries are consistently narrow, or low-latency indexing matters more than retrieval quality.

After collapsed tree search, pass the top-10 candidates through a cross-encoder reranker. The reranker picks the more precise node when a mid-level summary and a leaf chunk are both in the result set.
Frequently-updated corpora (summaries go stale), very short documents (flat chunking is already fine), or strict cost budgets on indexing.

# n_neighbors: higher = more global structure preserved.
# For RAG with ~100-500 chunks, n_neighbors=10-15 works well.
# For thousands of chunks, increase to 30-50.
reducer = UMAP(
    n_components=2,
    n_neighbors=15,      # locality vs globality
    min_dist=0.0,        # allow tight clusters
    metric="cosine",     # match your embedding distance metric
    random_state=42,
)

# A threshold of 0.5 means a chunk joins a cluster only if
# the GMM assigns it >50% probability. Lower thresholds
# (e.g. 0.1) increase overlap but inflate the number of
# nodes per cluster and may produce redundant summaries.
#
# Practical range: 0.3-0.6. Start at 0.5 and lower only if
# you see clusters missing boundary chunks.
MEMBERSHIP_THRESHOLD = 0.5
Building the tree is expensive for large corpora. Serialise and cache it.
pip install umap-learn scikit-learn langchain langchain-openai faiss-cpu
